coredata.dc:title: Construction of nickel oxide - carbon dots jasmine nano flowers derived from Sapindus Mukorossi for high performance supercapacitor applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kalaiselvi, K.
Prabhu, S.
Muthu Mareeswaran, Paulpandian
coredata.dc:description: Nickel oxide is now being developed as an electrode material for the storage of electrical charge due to its good chemical stability, cost-effectiveness, and higher thermal stability. “The nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nanoflowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF) are observed through “FESEM analysis”. The hydrothermal method is used to generate the nickel oxide-carbon dots jasmine nano flowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF), is assessed utilizing electrochemical tests such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). NiO and NiO-carbon dots-JNF have specific capacitance values of 823 F g−1 and 710 F g−1 at 5mVs−1. The three electrode system NiO-carbon dots-JNF GCD was exhibit remarkable electrochemical performance with coulombic efficiency (%) of 93.1 % at 1 A g−1 and cyclic stability over 10,000 cycles. In relation to this, we also investigated GCD curves using a two-electrode method, which demonstrated more cyclic stability over 15,000 cycles. The energy density is 32.85 Whkg−1 at 1 A g−1, power density is 5229 W Kg−1 at 15 A g−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel oxides
Supercapacitor
Carbon dots
Specific capacitance
Energy density
Power density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nano flower (NiO-Carbon dots-JNF)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the formation of nickel oxide- carbon dots jasmine nano flowers.
FE-SEM images of (a) NiO (b-e) NiO-carbon dots-JNF at various scale (b:100 nm; c:20 nm; d:2 μm; and e:1 μm) and (f) EDX spectrum of NiO-carbon dots-JNF.
HR-TEM images of (a and b) NiO-carbon dots-JNF (c) SAED pattern.
Raman spectral analysis of (a) NiO-carbon dots-JNF and (b) NiO.
XRD patterns of (a) NiO-carbon dots-JNF and (b) NiO.
(a) TGA analysis of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (b) DTA analysis of NiO-carbon dots-JNF.
(a) XPS spectra of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (b) Ni2p3 (c) O1s (d) C1s.
(a) Comparative CV profiles of NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO (b) CV profiles of NiO- carbon dots-JNF (c) Comparative GCD curves of NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO (d) GCD curves of NiO-carbon dots-JNF.

(a) Specific capacitance of the NiO-carbon dots and NiO materials at various current densities (b) Nyquist plot of NiO-carbon dots and NiO material (c) Equivalent circuit (d) Cyclic stability of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF material at 20 Ag−1 for 10,000 cycles.

(a) Hybrid supercapacitor device of specific capacitance of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF materials at various current densities (1–20 Ag−1) (b) GCD curves of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (c) Nyquist plots of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF material (d) Ragone plot of energy density versus power density of NiO-carbon dots-JNF material.
Specific capacitance data from CV and GCD studies of scan rate at 100 mVs−1.
Specific capacitance data from CV and GCD studies at various scan rates (From 5 mVs−1 to 100 mVs−1).
The resistance and capacitance values of the materials from Nyquist plots.
Comparison of specific capacitance of this work with the literature.

head.ce:title: Construction of nickel oxide - carbon dots jasmine nano flowers derived from Sapindus Mukorossi for high performance supercapacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Government Arts College for Women, Salem 636008, TN, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Government Arts College for Women, Salem-636008, TN, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Saveetha School of Engineering, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Saveetha University, Chennai 602 105, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Saveetha University, Chennai, TN, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering, Anna University, Chennai, TN, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering, Anna University, Chennai, TN, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel oxide is now being developed as an electrode material for the storage of electrical charge due to its good chemical stability, cost-effectiveness, and higher thermal stability. “The nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nanoflowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF) are observed through “FESEM analysis”. The hydrothermal method is used to generate the nickel oxide-carbon dots jasmine nano flowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF), is assessed utilizing electrochemical tests such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). NiO and NiO-carbon dots-JNF have specific capacitance values of 823 F g−1 and 710 F g−1 at 5mVs−1. The three electrode system NiO-carbon dots-JNF GCD was exhibit remarkable electrochemical performance with coulombic efficiency (%) of 93.1 % at 1 A g−1 and cyclic stability over 10,000 cycles. In relation to this, we also investigated GCD curves using a two-electrode method, which demonstrated more cyclic stability over 15,000 cycles. The energy density is 32.85 Whkg−1 at 1 A g−1, power density is 5229 W Kg−1 at 15 A g−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The NiO-carbon dots-JNF have provided encouraging outcomes for the development of supercapacitors. The development of supercapacitors using carbon quantum dots (C-dots) and graphene quantum dots has shown encouraging results [1]. The C-dots are good for catalysis, sensing, and supercapacitor applications due to their superior electrochemical and photochemical capabilities. Here, we propose a straightforward procedure for preparing NiO-carbon dots-JNF material. Our one-step method opens the possibility of using carbon dots on a large scale to develop electrode materials with high capacitance values for energy storage applications. The electrode materials, electrolyte, and separator are the three most crucial components of the supercapacitor. They have a large number of applications, including portable electronics; electric cars, and renewable energy systems, supercapacitors can deliver better power densities and longer life cycles than batteries as well as higher energy densities than conventional electric capacitors and energy storage devices. When compared to carbon-based materials, metal oxide electrodes exhibit a better power density and greater electrochemical stability [2–7]. NiO-based materials with wide surfaces and strong electrical conductivity have already been studied by Kalaiselvi et al. These characteristics make redox processes or faradic reactions, which have a maximum specific capacitance of 662Fg−1, more appropriate [8]. Ni/C battery applications are restricted the low power density and poor cyclic ability of NiO material. The micro-porous and branching structure can offer an extremely high surface area for the placement of nickel compounds which are electroactive. The most familiar electrodes are made of carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and activated carbons (ACs), are frequently cited for having desirable physical and chemical properties, controllable porosity and electro catalytic active sites in a variety of redox reactions [9]. The advantageous in-plane architecture offers the electrolyte ions lead to new opportunity to improve its contact with all the carbon layers. The NiO-carbon dots-JNF has provided encouraging outcomes for the development of supercapacitors. The development of supercapacitors using carbon quantum dots (C-dots) and graphene quantum dots has shown encouraging results. The C-dots are a good candidate for catalysis, sensing and supercapacitor applications due to their superior electrochemical and photochemical capabilities [10]. Here, we propose a straightforward procedure for preparing NiO-carbon dots-JNF material. Our one-step method opens the possibility of using carbon dots on a big scale to create electrode materials with high capacitance values for energy storage applications [11].

The Ni-Foam substrates, Nickel nitrate (98 % pure), copper nitrate (98 % pure) and urea (98 % pure) and NaOH were purchased from Merck and Loba Chemie, India. All the chemicals were used without further purification. De-ionized (DI) water was used throughout the study.

The collected sapindus mukorossi (SM) was placed in a clean jar and dried in the sun for 64 h before being processed to create SM fine powder for the carbon dots (C-dots). Then, at room temperature (311 °C), the SM powder samples were mixed for 30 min with a 1:1 mixture of water and ethanol. After 12 h of refluxing at 90 °C and the addition of a few drops of copra oil, the colour of mixture changed from brown to dark brown. The solution is cooled down and filtered after four hours, and the filtrate is identified as carbon dots.

The hydrothermal process was used to create the NiO nanoparticles. In the conventional hydrothermal procedure, a continual stream of stirring was used to add 1 M nickel nitrate hexahydrate Ni (NO3)2.6H2O (182.703 g/l), 0.4 M urea CO(NH2)2, and 0.4 M sodium hydroxide (1.6 g/l). After being transferred into the autoclave, the solution was maintained there for 12 h at 170 °C. After centrifuging the final product, it was dried for 30 min at 120 °C. To produce nickel oxide (NiO) materials, the product is annealed for 30 min at 650 °C.

The electrode was made by coating a slurry of active material (80 %), acetylene black (10 %), polyvinylidene fluoride (10 %), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidine solvent on pre-cleaned pressed nickel foam (NF). In a hot air oven, the coated electrode was dried for 6 h at 60 °C. About 1 mg of prepared components are bulk loaded into Ni foam. The prepared electrode served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl served as the reference electrode, and platinum wire served as the counter electrode throughout the electrochemical measurements, which were carried out in a three-electrode configuration. From the GCD curve, the specific capacitance and specific capacity of the electrodes were computed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nano flower (NiO-Carbon dots-JNF)
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal approach was used to produce NiO-carbon dots-JNF nanomaterials. The standard hydrothermal method uses 60 ml (46.86 g/L) of prepared carbon dots (CDs) solution along with 1 M nickel nitrate hexahydrate (NO3)2.6H2O (182.703 g/l), 0.4 M urea CO(NH2)2 (2.4 g/l), and 0.4 M NaOH (1.6 g/l). The solution was stirred for 90 min at room temperature (±31 °C) using a magnetic stirrer. After being put into a 150 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, the final solution was heated at 170 °C for 6 h. Ethanol and deionized water were used to wash the contaminants away. The product was then dried for a further 12 h at 120 °C in a hot air oven. The product was annealed at 350 °C for 30 min.

NiO-carbon dots-JNF were used as the positive electrode in a hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) while activated carbon AC / NiO-carbon dots-JNF served as the negative electrode and PVA- KOH is an electrolyte. The prepared electrode material was coated into acetylene black (10 %), PVDF (10 %), active substance (80 %), and added to 3 ml drops of N-Methyl prolidine (NMP) solvent. NiO-carbon dots-JNF and AC/ NiO-carbon dots-JNF both had loading doses of 1 mg and 2 mg, respectively.

The crystallite phase of the samples was examined using X-ray diffraction (Smart Lab, Rigaku Corporation Ltd). Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and field-emission SEM (FE-SEM) (Supra 40, Carl Zeiss) were used to investigate the morphology. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCA 3400 spectrometer) was used to study the binding energy, and raman measurements were engaged consuming (T64000, Jobin-Yvon, spectrometer fitted) with a liquid‑nitrogen-cooled CCD camera. The laser (Nd:YAG;532 nm) was employed as the excitation source, and the backscattered geometry was used to gather all the data.

The electrochemical characterization of a three-electrode system was characterized using an electrochemical workstation (SP150, BioLogic Science). An aqueous KOH 1 M alkaline electrolyte solution was used to throughout the work. Cyclic voltammetry was performed at various scan rates (5 mV/s to 100 mV/s). The cyclic voltammetry potential window range was fixed in the range of 0.05 V (versus Ag/AgCl). GCD curves are generated in this work from 1 to 15 A g−1. The frequency range of 100 Hz to 100 KHz was covered by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using an applied amplitude of 5 mV at an open circuit potential [12].
(1)
Specific capacity
Q = I × ∆ t / m
(2)
Specific capacitance
Csp
=
I ∆ t
m ∆ V
(3)
Specific capacitance
Csp
=
2
m
v 2
∫ iV
t
dt
(4)
Energy density =
Csp × ∆
v 2
7.2
wh / kg
(5)
Power density = Energy density ×
3600
∆ t
w kg
(6)
Coloumbic efficiency =
Discharging charging
× 100
(7)
Percentage porosity = 1 − db / dx × 100
(8)
Average crystallite size
D = kƛ / ꞵCosθ
where Csp is the specific capacitance (F g−1), I is the applied current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the active mass in the electrode (g), and ΔV is the working potential window measured (V) db and dx denotes bulk and x-ray density respectively [13].

In accordance with Fig. 2(e), this type of microstructure is advantageous for the redox phase's quick diffusion and easy accessibility, which leads to a high electrochemical capacitance [14]. Since the flowers are constructed of NiO-carbon dot and are woven by the NiO-carbon dot JNF [15–17].

Additionally, Fig. 2(f), displays the energy dispersive spectrum (EDAX) of NiO-Carbon dots-JNF. The elements such as carbon, oxygen and nickel are present according to the composition (Table 1 ). The carbon has a higher atomic percentage (70.22 %) than oxygen (28.47 %) due to the graphitization process.

Fig. 3(a), it displays the morphology of NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO materials. The NiO-carbon dots-JNF particle size is around 20 nm and the shape is spherical [18]. From the Fig. 3(b), the selected area of diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates the polycrystalline nature and the spots are inferred as graphitized carbon dots [19].

The interplanar and lattice fringes of NiO-carbon dots-JNF are displayed in Fig. 3(c). The red arrows indicate the existence of NiO material of carbon dots on the surface and the corresponding d spacing values (0.11 nm, 0.13 nm and 0.14 nm) are attributed to the preferred orientation of (111), (220) and (200) of NiO.

The chemical structures, phases and molecular interactions of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO are derived from Raman spectra as shown in Fig. 4 .(a, b). The D-band at 1394 cm−1 appears as the effect of the deformation of sp2 carbon atoms by the oxygen groups in the composite and the G-band at 1572 cm−1, which is a consequence of the stretching of the CC bond brought on by the carbon dots and the two well-resolved distinctive peaks in the Raman spectrum of NiO-carbon dots-JNF [20,21]. The D and G bands (D: associated with defects; G: Graphitized carbon dots) are represented by two shifts that are detected at 1394 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1.

The charge transfer occurs between carbon and NiO material through carbon dots by observing G-band shifts of 1394 cm−1 and 1572 cm−1. In this, the NiO vibrational peak is appeared at 1394 cm−1 and the strong vibrational peak at 1572 cm−1. It is demonstrated by the (Id/Ig = 0.86) result of NiO-carbon dots-JNF [22]. The NiO-carbon dots-JNF material exhibits sp2 hybridization and has Raman shift at 1394 cm−1 (d peak). Therefore, NiO-Carbon dots-JNF (5.565 × 10−3) has greater electrical conductivity than NiO material (3.065 × 10−3). According to Fig. 4(b), NiO is exhibits a vibrational mode called A1g, which relates to the symmetric stretching brought by the incidence of oxygen atoms in NiO and has a wavelength of 464 cm−1.

X-ray diffraction is an effective tool for characterizing the crystalline segments of the material. Fig. 5(a), displays the XRD pattern of NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO (JCPDS card number (78–0643)) materials [8]. The preferred orientations of NiO-carbon dot-JNF are well congruent: (111), (200), (220) and (311) at 2θ positions of 37.26°, 43.29°, 62.88°, 75.42°. The atomic percentage of porosity (29.5 %) has been calculated using XRD data for NiO-Carbon dots-JNF material [23].

NiO is indexed in its preferred orientations, including (111), (200), (220) and (311) located at 2θ positions of 37.26°, 43.29°, 62.88°, 75.42°. This is depicted in Fig. 5(b). The peaks of NiO are observed in (Fig. 5.4(a), and the representative peaks of carbon dots are also appeared (2θ = 14.4° and 26.4°) [24]. It is obvious that all these materials are having a strong carbon dot stacking peaks at 14.4° and 26.4°. The preferred orientations of NiO-carbon dots-JNF are (002) and (001), as mentioned in Fig. 5(b), (JCPDS card number 78–0643) [25].

The weight loss of NiO/carbon dots-JNF material was monitored by thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) as shown in Fig. 6(a) at 660 °C. The NiO-carbon dots-JNF experiences total weight loss of only 2 % and 98 % of the original material remains stable between 300 °C and 600 °C [26].
From Fig. 6(b), it depicts the DTA analysis of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF impregnated material, which shows three areas with significant weight losses that can be interpreted as follows:

As a result of the dehydration of water from the sample weight loss occurs at temperatures from 300 °C to 660 °C. The weight loss is in region (I) is 0.5 % of its initial value. But in the case of region II, the minimum weight loss is observed as 450 °C then compared to region III as (600 °C) [27].

The chemical state of each element in NiO-carbon dots-JNF was investigated using X- ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique. The presence of Ni, O and C components on the materials surface is depicted in Fig. 7(a). No other peaks were found as impurities other then carbon dots. According to the XPS data, the carbon dots increased the conductivity of NiO material [28].

From Fig. 7(b), the Ni2p spectra of the samples showed the primary components that are found at 853 eV (NiI) and 872 eV (NiII). They can be recognized to the core levels such as Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2, with energy split-up among the double peaks of about 19 eV. This confirms the oxidation state of the Ni ion, which occurs in +2 valence state, and it is indicated the presence of coulombic interaction between core holes and valence electrons. NiO-carbon dots-JNF (19 eV) has the binding energy that is substantially higher than NiO.

The high-resolution O1s spectrum was further deconvoluted into two peaks as shown in Fig. 7(c). The locations of these peaks are 529(OI) and 531(OII). Among them, the lesser binding energy peak is designated as OI and it is linked to oxygen atoms that form the bonds with metal ions (ONi), whereas the C-Ni-O bond, with an associated binding energy of 529 eV results from the formation of Ni-O-C bond. The oxygen vacancy is linked to the peak which is labelled as OI in the core level spectra and the surface adsorption of oxygen species (O2−, CO3 2− and H2O) was designated as oxygen [29].

Additionally, an oxygen vacancy-related peak appeared at 531 eV. Since these analyses are completed on oxide materials that have remained exposed to ambient water vapor and they were tested for any potential oxygen vacancies. Ni2p may bind to functional clusters on the surface of NiO-carbon dots-JNF that include oxygen or carbon atoms, depending on the situation. The C-OH and/or C-O-C (hydroxyl and/or epoxy) groups are responsible for the peak at 531 eV.

In accumulation, the peak at 531 eV (OII) in the spectrum of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF is expressively smaller when compared to the C-OH and/or C-O-C groups in the composite, which recommends that hydrogen has been replaced by oxygen in hydroxyl groups or that an epoxy ring reaction with Ni2p in NiO may have formed the C-O-Ni linkage [30].

The high-resolution C1s spectra are further deconvoluted into three peaks Fig. 7(d). These peaks are located at of 285 eV and 286 eV. The peak at 285 eV can be attributed to the carbon dots bound to the nickel (NiC) in a sub-stoichiometric Ni3C phase and it is known as NiC. It is included as the contribution from C or Ni atoms in the C- Ni bond. The presence of CC or NiC is related to the peak that is observed at 285 eV. This shows a mixed composition of carbon and nickel (6:1) and the C1s, which appears at 285 eV, is attributed to CO bonding structure. The amount of NiO compound is reduces when the carbon fraction in the NiO-carbon dots-JNF grows, whereas the amount of graphitized carbon (Fig. 5.6(a)) increases [31].

Fig. 8(a) displays the CV profiles of nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nano flowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF) and NiO scan rate at a 5 mVs−1 in 1 M KOH electrolyte. NiO-carbon dots-JNF has a cathodic peak at 0.11 V (oxidation potential) and an anodic peak at 0.19 V (reduction potential). It is evident that when scan rate increases, the anodic peaks shift forwards positive potential side while the cathodic peaks do the opposite [32]. NiO-carbon dots-JNF has greater current density (15 mAcm−2) than that of NiO material (10 mAcm−2). From the Fig. 8(b), the CV curves of NiO-carbon dots-JNF generated at various scan rates (from 5 mVs−1 to 100 mVs−1).

It is visible as prominent redox peaks for the faradaic reactions of Ni2+ ions [33]. In particular, the electrode becomes more stable and the current density is enhanced with the scan rate. When compared to NiO material, the NiO-carbon dots-JNF material exhibits a greater integral area because of its better electric conductivity [34], which may result in carbonized jasmine nano flowers and indicate a high degree of reversibility of the quick charge and slow discharge reaction. The oxidation of NiO-carbon dots-JNF into NiO (charging) and the reduction of Ni-O-OH into NiO (discharging) result in the appearance of the anodic and cathodic peaks. The following redox reaction occurs in the process.
(1)
NiO +
OH −
↔ NiOOH +
e −

The GCD investigation is utilized to investigate for NiO and NiO-carbon dots-JNF (1 Ag−1) at a potential window of (0–0.4 V), as shown in Fig. 8(c). The NiO-carbon dots-JNF has a greater specific capacitance (825 Fg−1) than that of NiO (710 Fg−1) at 1 Ag−1. The GCD investigation, which was conducted by utilizing NiO-carbon dots-JNF at a range of current densities including 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15 and 20 Ag−1 at a constant potential window (0–0.4 V) are shown in Fig. 8(d) (Tables 2 and 3
).

The specific capacitances of NiO-carbon dots-JNF with NiO materials were presented in relation to current density (from 1 Ag−1 to 20 Ag−1) as shown in Fig. 9(a). The capacitive controlled electrochemical processes cause the specific capacitance of NiO to decrease with applied current densities (from1Ag−1 to 20Ag−1). These findings establish the synergistic interaction between NiO and carbon dots species that increases adsorbed surface area by modifying the Ni/C ratio. It has a considerable impact on the materials redox reactions [35–37].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the charge transfer resistance of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF and NiO materials at frequencies between 100 Hz and 100 kHz. The materials perform better in terms of capacitance and lower resistance at lower frequencies [38,39]. A more efficient electron-transport pathway, with lower ion diffusion resistance is suggested by the smaller semicircle. The fitted parameters, such as solution resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode/electrolyte interface are shown in Fig. 9(b,c) and also reported in Table 4 .

NiO (107 Ωcm−2) has substantially higher internal resistance (Rs) value than that of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (0.55 Ωcm−2). Similarly, NiO charge transfer resistance (Rct) (3.065 × 10−3) value is lower than that of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (5.565 × 10−3). The uniformly distributed NiO carbon dots on the graphitized carbon of the jasmine nano flowers caused the increased electrical conductivity of NiO-carbon dots-JNF materials, which speeds up the redox reactions by allowing the ions from the electrode/electrolyte solution to diffuse into the interior bulk surface of the materials [40]. According to Fig. 9(d), a high current density of 20 Ag−1 was used to examine the cyclic stability of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF material. The NiO-carbon dots-JNF materials capacity retention for (88 %) of 10,000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 20 Ag−1 and a coulombic efficiency of 93.1 %. The capacitance retention was achieved and it is inferred that the material is very potent for the future industrial applications [41–43].

From the Fig. 10 (a), displays the CV profiles of nickel oxide carbon dots jasmine nano flowers (NiO-carbon dots-JNF/AC) at a scan rate of 5 mVs−1 and potential (from 0 V to 1.4 V). The cyclic voltammetry curves, current density increase upon increases the scan rates and shape displays the fast ion and electron transfer with good reversibility in hybrid supercapacitor device [44]. Further, the GCD profiles of the hybrid supercapacitor device were tested at various current densities (1to 10Ag−1) and potential (from0 V to1.4 V) are shown in Fig. 10(b). NiO- carbon dots-JNF has a remarkable specific capacity of 250 Cg−1 at 1 Ag−1.

Fig. 10 (c), the Nyquist plot of the NiO-carbon dots-JNF material illustrated to the ideal capacitive behavior. Fig. 10 (d), the Ragone plot of energy density versus power density of NiO-carbon dots-JNF/AC material delivered the maximum energy density of 32 Whkg−1 at a power density of 5229 Wkg−1 (Table 5 ).

The NiO-carbon dots-JNF was prepared in a single process, and an X-ray diffraction (XRD) investigation exposes that they are polycrystalline in nature. The sharing of NiO-carbon dots-JNF on the carbonized jasmine nano flowers primarily depends on the carbon dot concentration, which is in agreement with the morphology. Raman shift for NiO-carbon dots-JNF (Id/Ig =0.86) can be charity to determine whether the carbon has been graphitized. The cyclic voltammetry consequences proposal that the current density of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (25mAcm−2) is superior than NiO material (20 mA cm−2). Galvanic charge- discharge investigation of the specific capacitance of NiO-carbon dots-JNF (823F g−1) and NiO (710F g−1) shows that NiO-carbon dots-JNF has a higher specific capacitance than NiO. According to the EIS results, NiO-carbon dots-JNF has a lower resistance (0.55 Ω cm−2) than NiO (107 Ω cm−2). This study claimed that NiO-carbon dots-JNF had a more effective electron-transport route than NiO. With a coulombic efficiency of 93.1 % and capacity retention of 90 % after 10,000 repeated charge-discharge cycles, and three/two-electrode technique, this recognized more cyclic stability over 15,000 cycles. The energy density is 32.85 Whkg−1 at 1 A g−1 power density is 5229 W Kg−1 at 15 A g−1.
K. Kalaiselvi: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. S. Prabhu: Software, Data curation. Paulpandian Muthu Mareeswaran: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The reconstruction of char surface by oxidized quantum-size carbon dots under the ultrasonic energy to prepare modi fi ed activated carbon materials as electrodes for supercapacitors
J. Alloys Compd.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Leng, K. Sun, J. Li, J. Jiang, The reconstruction of char surface by oxidized quantum-size carbon dots under the ultrasonic energy to prepare modi fi ed activated carbon materials as electrodes for supercapacitors, Journal of Alloys and Compounds. 714 (2017) 443–452. doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.04.204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Binder-free MOF-based and MOF-derived Nanoarrays for flexible electrochemical energy storage: Progress and perspectives
Small

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc selenide/cobalt selenide in nitrogen-doped carbon frameworks as anode materials for high-performance sodium-ion hybrid capacitors
